item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I absolutely love this, so far this is one of my favorite SCP’s yet. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, its refreshing to see a humanoid SCP that helps out and can be seen in a semi-positive light. There are plenty of malicious and evil SCPs out there so its change of pace to see this. All in all this was definitely a good read. One thing I would say about it was the seeming lax of the professional tone to the SCP from the staff, but that's just me anyways. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, but there’s no point in drawing a picture to avoided sexualization. Ever heard of hentai? Photography and drawing are both arts with ways to create end products with and without sexualization, so this is just unprofessional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I disagree on the artistic representation. It made it feel more like an actual experience a normal person had meeting this anomaly and then trying to draw a picture of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love it. It's a unique enough spin on the concept of a "good" SCP using the element of sarkicism to make it a well paced and enjoyable read throughout. Even though I did find it thoroughly predictable, it didn't make the article boring or unreadable in any capacity. The one thing I found a little immersion breaking was the picture, but I understand the vision you were going for with it, and it doesn't reduce the quality of the article too much.

Overall just a really solid read, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In all fairness, hidden text appears to be expected here and I've seen more than a few complaints when this type of information isn't placed that way in the comments for other SCPs. The author may be in a damned if you do, damned if you don't position.

I know every tine I post a comment longer than a few sentences, I wonder when I'm going to get a nastygram telling me collapse some of it.

EDIT:As Zyn says in a comment above, collapsed text may be requested as a courtesy to those reading on mobile devices.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had a similar idea for improving the image. I was going to suggest finding a cc or otherwise copyright free CAT scan or MRI coronal and/pr saggital images of the chest and abdomen. Erase the organs and put in some slices of arm bones. Because we will be looking at slices of the arm bones, it doesn't require much artistry. The long bones of the arm are not hard to imagine in section as they are pretty much just cylinders. It could even be coupled with a drawing like Rounzie's for clarity.

Such an image would avoid nudity, not require much artistic skill, and yet really up the scientific verisimilitude of the article. I think it would be a huge plus. If I get some time maybe I can cook up at least an example of what I mean.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like cybersqyd, I liked the concept of portraying the people involved with sarkicism in a deeper and more nuanced manner and less "always chaotic evil". And like cybersqyd I have some issues with the dialogue, just different ones.

I'm not bothered by the fact that it was almost certain that she was going to take up the offer eventually. Nor was I bothered by the healing hero aspect. I'm not a surgeon but I am a physician so I've known many and plenty of surgeons see themselves this way. And actually the dialogue read a lot like what I might expect from an idealistic med student, and that is my issue.

If she was a surgeon, she went through not only med school but a mentally and physically grueling and often frankly abusive residency. Possibly a fellowship on top of it.

Classic med school joke (mild profanity)

+ show block
– hide block

What's the difference between a medical student and dog shit? Nobody goes out of their way to step on dog shit.

And surgery has an unfortunately deserved reputation of being sexist and especially abusive and hard on women. SCP-4197 went through all that and thrived. But she often reads like an med student needing reassurance. She meekly inquires about how Dr Turner is doing when she there should be undertones of "of course Marcus is going to be OK." She might be arrogant and dismissive of others or she could be gracious and even humble. Both are consistent with being a surgeon. But no matter what, she should radiate confidence in her abilities.

Even so, I liked it enough to give it +1 as is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really, really want to like this. But there’s simply no reason for an artistic representation or hidden text.

-1 until the hidden text is integrated into the main story and artistic representation removed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Scroll to the bottom of the page, find the "+ Options" link, click that. There should be a "Page Source" option you can click.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know how to check the page source. How do I do that? Or can you send a link so I can read the hidden part of this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As with the other commenters, please at least reference the hidden text. Its not like it adds anything to the skip itself tbqh.

I agree as well that 4197 just isn't an interesting entity. She doesn't have any internal conflicts that a Mother Theresa figure would have, and seems almost like a caricature of the "I'm a healing HERO!"

I don't see what the point of the tentacles were, I don't see how her having a hollow chest adds anything to the piece. I want to like this, but its just…

It feels so incomplete. I can see some parallels here to 073, as a SCP that directly works for Foundation interests, being Foundation staff in everything but name and designation, but unless you add tales, supplementary material, just…anything at all to add to this, I can't find this anomaly really interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Likewise, the drawing has absolutely not changed my vote. I fully understand the technical limitations of the author and not everyone can create high quality artwork or photoshops.

The picture works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this. It was an interesting read. The do gooder morality is in start contrast with what we read about most sarkism, so much so that I wonder about her ignorance based on as you noted her specific teacher despite her seeming expertise. But thats kind of in a different direction than what you'd laid out. And I like articles that aren't all just depressing and dark.

I do agree with other commenters that the hidden text should be unhidden in some way. This doesn't look like it would be a formatscrew and so I did not have and suspicions about it till I read the comments. Maybe a semi-hidden link to an alt list pages portion? Or a link in a footnote? I think something that leaves some sort of visible trail would be helpful.

Also I agree you should either create or commission a more scientific/anatomical picture (or just a skeletal depiction) or just take it out. Some kind of more scientific visual element would help a lot. I think it detracts from the article as is. For example I spent 30 minutes using a couple CC images from the noun project and used GIMP to make this skeletal diagram(http://scp-sandbox-3.wdfiles.com/local--files/rounzie/4197). I think you could have a much better visual with this article without a huge investment in time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, I never intended to imply that this was the site's opinion or any form of general consensus. This is just my personal opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm just going to post a comment here saying i agree with Dyslexion more than AugumentedSpartan here, just because this chain of comments feels too one-sided to me, and i want to say that this isn't the opinion of the whole site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for pointing out the obvious: I am not Leonardo Da Vinci. That's a good thing to get out of the way. It would be really weird if my art was on par with like, one of the Renaissance masters because I probably wouldn't be on this site if it was.

I wasn't trying to be derogatory, I was pointing out that it is very jarring to see an amateur drawing in what is otherwise "scientific" fiction. I obviously don't expect you to be da Vinci, the same way I don't expect PhD level physics in a gravitational SCP, but if said gravitational article has wonky basic physics, then it's better to not have it at all since it hurts the article. Having a sketch here is the same, the lack of it would be better. Here's a better example of what is seen in scientific drawings. It is a "sketchy" style, but there is a lot of detail and precision in order to get it as close to the truth as possible.

+ show block
– hide block

From a meta-perspective, this was included because I encountered a lot of people who wondered how her appendages worked. Here's the answer. As for the sexualization, my resources for figure models are limited, so I used some old pics as reference.

You could have simply put it on a comment as an out of universe reference. You did hide part of the story in the source code, after all. We wouldn't find that unless we read the comments.

In-universe, while they dehumanize SCPs in documentation they still know they're humans. So including nude photographs would be iffy, in my mind.

An understandable view, but even without it being a photograph, she's still sexualized. Real medical pictures aren't actually sexualized, they feel very neutral. Kinda sterile, in a way.

But I choose to depict 4197 in a somewhat sexual position and at this level of skill, to make the reader see that that's how Doctor Turner sees her. The definition of the body vs the lack of it on the appendages is meant to give you the idea that he's focused on her normal parts. When you get to the interviews, He says a lot that he admires what she did, and he intervenes when she's in her depressed state. There's a decent amount of rapport and care in his lines to her, because Doctor Turner is attracted to her, and the picture is supposed to reflect that. Plus he's in charge of this document, so its his call what picture goes up.

That came to mind after I read the interviews, but it still feels like it doesn't work. The in-universe reason seems weird because more than just Dr. Turner would see the page, such as Dr. Virgil, and having a sexualized drawing of the SCP would easily raise several eyebrows, and would most likely be reported.

The meta problem is that the concept isn't really explored. Take SCP-847 or the rewrite of SCP-166. Both of those explore the sexualization of female characters both in writing itself, as well as the in-universe aspects, in depth as analysis of the inherent problem about that trope, but here it doesn't go past basic character interactions. Sure, Dr. Turner likes 4197, but there isn't much of a follow up on how that affects her containment, his job, or the general rules of the Foundation. It feels too superficial for it to excuse a common problem found in media.

I really like this bit of UraniumEmpire's comment in his SCP-166 rewrite (and his comment in general, really):

There's points to be made about the diegetic implications of a piece, and how that can break something in two. Such points, however, must concede that they are extensions of the author's will. Anything put to paper was put there through the direction of a creator.

It's true that you wrote a reason for the drawing to be there, but you chose to add the drawing, not the character since he isn't real. So whatever the reason in-universe is, this piece still has an image of an objectified woman, which I believed should be properly explored in the article for it to be there. Otherwise it's the same as the "she chose to dress slutty" argument.


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hello! Thank you for pointing out the obvious: I am not Leonardo Da Vinci. That's a good thing to get out of the way. It would be really weird if my art was on par with like, one of the Renaissance masters because I probably wouldn't be on this site if it was.

From a meta-perspective, this was included because I encountered a lot of people who wondered how her appendages worked. Here's the answer. As for the sexualization, my resources for figure models are limited, so I used some old pics of myself as reference.

In-universe, while they dehumanize SCPs in documentation they still know they're humans. So including nude photographs would be iffy, in my mind.

I could have drawn this in any style, and I could have easily taken it to a much more artistic level, (I've been doing this for years.) But I choose to depict 4197 in a somewhat sexual position and at this level of skill, to make the reader see that that's how Doctor Turner sees her. The definition of the body vs the lack of it on the appendages is meant to give you the idea that he's focused on her normal parts. When you get to the interviews, He says a lot that he admires what she did, and he intervenes when she's in her depressed state. There's a decent amount of rapport and care in his lines to her, because Doctor Turner is attracted to her, and the picture is supposed to reflect that. Plus he's in charge of this document, so its his call what picture goes up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't not talk about the image, it's too glaring for me to ignore, both in a meta and in-universe sense.

In the meta sense, I assume that a part of it is to avoid having nudity in an article as well as not sexualizing the anomaly (this last bit I'm taking from the "ethical concerns" bit) but I can't see why you couldn't just have no image at all. First of all, if a scientific document had to have drawings rather than photographs, the drawings would have to be detailed and professional and, no offence, your drawing isn't up to par. As for the sexualization in the meta sense, assuming this was a reasoning so feel free to correct me, it feels contradictory to give her large breasts, a skinny frame, and a modelling pose. I would expect the sketch to have a neutral stance rather than the cover of a fashion magazine. It would also have made more sense to not have her be conventionally attractive. As it is, it feels like she's being sexualized even if there isn't any actual nudity.

From the point of view in-universe, I honestly can't imagine why the Ethics Committee would not approve of pictures. If we assume that the articles are just the summary of the anomaly and that there are a lot more notes and experiments in the database (would make sense since a lot of articles have a "see X for more information" and the nature of the site itself) then there would be plenty of nude shots of humanoid anomalies for medical and research purposes. Hospitals in real life do that to document physical damage. Not only that, the Foundation already dehumanizes anomalies (calling them "it") and keeps them locked against their will, not to mention the experimentation itself. So nude photographs for the purpose of documentation seems par for the course.

It just takes me out of the story to see what looks like a sketch of someone's "sexy" OC in what is supposed to be a serious piece of fiction in a scientific setting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ spoilers?
– hide block

Check the page source, read the missing section and then come back.

…are you intending for the average reader to read this section? There's nothing in the text to hint at it's existence really so it seems likely that they wouldn't see it (as indeed, I didn't).

It kinda feels, perhaps a little too important to leave hidden like this; but also like it perhaps needs to be a little more clearly tied into the SCP if you were to just, unhide it?

I kinda feel like the conflict between healing people weakening her and her being determined to help folk anyway could be a little clearer? Interview 3 led me towards thinking the weakness was more "she's not eating enough/taking care of herself well enough" than "the healing specifically is weakening her" (well, beyond a "healing takes up energy" way? Like in the sense that healing like exercise and running while hungry is bad for you?).

Iunno; it definitely feels like you have a good concept here and also like it's not quite landing right for me :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
thanks for reading!

Spoilers
<3

Check the page source, read the missing section and then come back.


I am gonna spoil this but here's why she never questions the right thing, or tries to escape. The Karcist who trained her was originally more in line with the general sarkic beliefs until that devotion cost her her relationship with her daughter. Since she loved her daughter more than the road she was on, she changed to try to be a better person. It didn't work, but the Karcist never gave up and keep trying, until we get to Sarah.

Sarah isn't a perfect person. What she is, is heavily indoctrinated to an ideology. And while it is moral, her surrogate mother figure did impress it upon her from age six on. Like Virgil mentioned in the final interview, she can't say no to her morality and personal ethics, even if it's not good for her. Healing people severely weakens her. Everything she does is because of this Karcist's attempts at atonement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really dig the core idea here and the like, decision to portray sarkicism in a more positive light but at the same time, the dialogue just, doesn't quite work for me?

+ expanded thoughts
– hide block

It feels perhaps a bit too predictable and flat for me: there's no real narrative tension and the characters don't quite feel interesting enough to carry this; so it just feels, a bit too much like it's hammering home the same points for a long time.

I think it doesn't help that SCP-4197 feels,,,too perfect? She never questions doing the right thing or anything: it never even seems to occur to her that she could escape or not help the Foundation during the containment breach. Her decision to help the Foundation feels inevitable as soon as it's put on the table; and I can't help but feel that if this ended after the second interview, it'd feel stronger.

Then again, my opinion here seems to be in the minority so feel free to leave this as it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The character arc hews a little too closely to Iris's in Resurrection, but that's ultimately not a bad thing, and your justification for using a drawing instead of a picture is exceedingly clever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
reserved

art by me
